coredata.dc:title: Novel polysaccharide-derived carbon dots doped with N, S, P: Synthesis, characterization, in vitro antioxidant, fluorescence sensor for chromium (VI) detection and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Baijian
Chen, Zihao
Li, Na
Bi, Yongguang
Kong, Fansheng
Wang, Zhong
Tan, Shaofan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, for the first time, biomass-derived carbon dots (AMP-CDs) with strong water solubility, antioxidant activity and good biocompatibility were synthesized by a one-step microwave method using Allium macrostemon Bunge polysaccharides (AMP) as the main carbon source, L-cysteine as the nitrogen and sulfur source, and phosphoric acid as the phosphorus source. The results showed that sphere-like AMP-CDs with an average particle size of about 2.34 ± 0.49 nm were successfully prepared, with good resistance to salt and photobleaching, and maintained high fluorescence intensity under normal pH conditions in human body. AMP-CDs were highly resistant to 2,2-diphenyl-1-pycrylhydrazyl (DPPH), 2,2′-Azinobis- (3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate) (ABTS) and hydroxyl radical (·OH) with significantly higher scavenging ability than AMP, with IC50 (μg/mL) of 10.240, 7.265, and 574.730, respectively. In addition, AMP-CDs can be selectively used for the sensitive detection of Cr6+ with the limit of detection was 5.25 μM, and satisfactory results were obtained for the Cr6+ recovery experiments of actual environmental samples. In vitro cytotoxicity and hemolytic activity assays showed that AMP-CDs have good biocompatibility and can smoothly enter cells for clear imaging. Therefore, AMP-CDs are likely expected to be applied as a potential antioxidant and metal ion detection probe in the field of drug and food, environmental and biological scenario samples for metal ion detection analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polysaccharide
Simple synthesis
N, S, P-doped carbon dots
Antioxidant
Cr (VI) detection
Intracellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Biocompatibility assay and cellular imaging of AMP-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by Yunfu Traditional Chinese Medicine (Southern Medicine) Innovation Team Project , Guangdong Provincial Doctoral Workstation (No. 192 ), Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou Science and Technology Bureau (No. 202102080365 ), Yunfu City Science and Technology Plan Project (No. 2020020101 ), Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Science and Technology Department (No. KTP20200170 ), Project of Traditional Chinese Medicine Bureau of Guangdong Province (No. 20231422 ), Zhongshan Social Welfare and basic research program (No. 2020B2005 ), Project of Guangdong Provincial Department of Education-special projects in key areas (No. 2021ZDZX4013 ), Heyuan City Science and Technology Plan Project (No. 2018014 ) and Guangdong MEPP Fund (No. GDOE[2019]A27 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of (A) microwave power, (B) mass ratio and (C) time on the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs. (D–F) Response surface 3D plots showing the significant interactive effect of microwave-assisted synthesis variables on the AMP-CDs' fluorescence intensity of system (n = 3).
(A) SEM image of AMP. (B–D) SEM-EDS image and analysis of AMP-CDs. (E) TEM images of AMP-CDs. (F) Particle size distribution of AMP-CDs. The inset in (E) is the high-resolution TEM images of AMP-CDs.
(A) Full-scan XPS spectrum of AMP-CDs. (B) C1s, (C) N1s, (D) O1s, (E) P2p and (F) S2s high-resolution XPS spectra of AMP-CDs. The inset in (A) is the element percentage image of AMP-CDs.

(A) FT-IR spectra for AMP and AMP-CDs. (B) UV–vis absorption of AMP (blue) and AMP-CDs (black) and photoluminescence excitation (purple) and emission (orange) spectra of AMP-CDs, λex = 380 nm and λem = 465 nm (inset: image of AMP-CDs at daylight on the left, image of AMP-CDs at UV light (365 nm) on the right). (C) The fluorescence spectrum of AMP-CDs under different excitation wavelengths (inset: corresponding normalized spectra). (D) Linear relationship between the UV absorption of AMP-CDs (black) and Integrated FL intensity and the linear relationship between the UV absorption of Quinine sulphate (black) and Integrated FL intensity (n = 3).
Antioxidant activities of AMP, AMP-CDs, and Vc in vitro. (A): DPPH radical; (B): ABTS radical; (C): hydroxyl radical (n = 3).

Effect of (A) pH, (B) time and (C) temperature on the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs. (D-E) Response surface 3D plots showing the significant interactive effect of detection of Cr6+ variables on the AMP-CDs' fluorescence intensity of the system (n = 3).

(A) Selectivity for various metal ions and detection of Cr6+ in the mixture. (B) UV–vis absorption of AMP-CDs (black), AMP-CDs + Cr6+ (green), and Cr6+ (red). (C) Fluorescence decay traces of AMP-CDs and with the addition of Cr6+ (150 μM). (D) FL spectra of the AMP-CDs response to Cr6+ ions with different concentrations (0-500 μM). (E) The linear plots of F0/F versus the concentration of Cr6+ in the range from 0 to 500 mM (F0 and F represent the PL intensity of the initial and added Cr6+, respectively). (F) Stern Volmer curve for AMP-CDs@Cr6+ system (n = 3).

(A) Effects of AMP-CDs on the viability of 4T1 cells (n = 3). (B): Hemolysis percentages of AMP-CDs (a: P > 0.05; inset: AMP-CDs with different concentrations and 2 % red blood cells after incubation, n = 3). (C) Fluorescence image (405 nm), (D) fluorescence image (488 nm), (E) bright field image and (F) merged image of 4T1 cells. (G) Fluorescence image (405 nm), (H) fluorescence image (488 nm), (I) bright field image and (J) merged image of RAW264.7 cells.
The IC50 (μg/mL) of AMP-CDs and AMP on DPPH, ABTS and hydroxyl free radicals scavenging effects.
Comparison of different methods for the determination of Cr6+.
Determination of Cr6+ in real water and plant samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Novel polysaccharide-derived carbon dots doped with N, S, P: Synthesis, characterization, in vitro antioxidant, fluorescence sensor for chromium (VI) detection and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Drug Delivery, Guangdong Provincial Engineering Center of Topical Precise Drug Delivery System, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Drug Delivery
Guangdong Provincial Engineering Center of Topical Precise Drug Delivery System

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Drug Delivery, Guangdong Provincial Engineering Center of Topical Precise Drug Delivery System, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Dongshenglin Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Dongshenglin Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunfu Traditional Chinese Medicine Hospital, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yunfu Traditional Chinese Medicine Hospital, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Pharmacy, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou 510006, Guangdong, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, for the first time, biomass-derived carbon dots (AMP-CDs) with strong water solubility, antioxidant activity and good biocompatibility were synthesized by a one-step microwave method using Allium macrostemon Bunge polysaccharides (AMP) as the main carbon source, L-cysteine as the nitrogen and sulfur source, and phosphoric acid as the phosphorus source. The results showed that sphere-like AMP-CDs with an average particle size of about 2.34 ± 0.49 nm were successfully prepared, with good resistance to salt and photobleaching, and maintained high fluorescence intensity under normal pH conditions in human body. AMP-CDs were highly resistant to 2,2-diphenyl-1-pycrylhydrazyl (DPPH), 2,2′-Azinobis- (3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate) (ABTS) and hydroxyl radical (·OH) with significantly higher scavenging ability than AMP, with IC50 (μg/mL) of 10.240, 7.265, and 574.730, respectively. In addition, AMP-CDs can be selectively used for the sensitive detection of Cr6+ with the limit of detection was 5.25 μM, and satisfactory results were obtained for the Cr6+ recovery experiments of actual environmental samples. In vitro cytotoxicity and hemolytic activity assays showed that AMP-CDs have good biocompatibility and can smoothly enter cells for clear imaging. Therefore, AMP-CDs are likely expected to be applied as a potential antioxidant and metal ion detection probe in the field of drug and food, environmental and biological scenario samples for metal ion detection analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots are a new type of zero-dimensional material with a particle size of <10 nm, stable properties, less toxicity and good biocompatibility [1]. Carbon dots are promising carbon nanomaterials with applications [2–5] in fluorescent probes, contaminant detection, bioimaging, photocatalysis, drug delivery, etc. Generally speaking, carbon dots are synthesized by top-down and bottom-up methods [6,7]. The former mainly consists of physical, chemical or electrochemical methods as dividing large carbon structures into obtain smaller size carbon dots, such as electrochemical methods, arc discharge methods and laser ablation methods; the latter mainly transforms small molecules or polymer precursors to obtain carbon dots through coalescence-crosslinking‑carbonization processes, such as hydrothermal/solvent thermal, combustion/pyrolysis, supercritical fluid, electrochemical treatment methods, etc. [8,9]. All the above methods have certain limitations, such as low production yield, long synthesis time, high cost and toxic chemicals as raw materials for synthesis. The microwave synthesis method has advantages of green, simple, safe and short time limit, which is an efficient and sustainable application method [10,11]. Carbon dots can also be made from natural plants [12,13] as carbon sources, such as cabbage, sweet red pepper, aloe vera, perilla and ginger. Bio-based derived carbon dots [14] generally have good biosafety due to the natural and low toxicity of their raw materials, and the synthetic raw materials are inexpensive, widely available and rich in elements. However, the raw materials used in the bio-based carbon dots synthesis mentioned above are not chemically explicit, and their plausibility and reproducibility are not as ideal.

Polysaccharides [15] are a class of macromolecular compounds linked by glycosidic bonds, which are abundant in nature and can be classified into plant polysaccharides, animal polysaccharides, microbial polysaccharides and synthetic polysaccharides according to their sources. Polysaccharides have complex branching structures and active hydroxyl groups, and have biological activities [16,17] such as antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, antitumor, antioxidant, hypoglycemic, regulation of intestinal flora, modulation of immune activity, etc. Moreover, natural polysaccharides have good biocompatibility, biodegradability, low toxicity, etc., and are very suitable as precursors for the synthesis of biological materials. At present, polysaccharides have been widely used in medicine, health food and clinical, and become a hot spot in the field of food science, natural medicine and life science [18]. However, the biological activity of some polysaccharides is not so prominent. Thus, many methods of modifying polysaccharides have been derived to improve the biological activity, such as esterification, etherification, oxidation, oxidation, amidation, etc. [19,20]. Compared to the traditional ways of modifying polysaccharides as mentioned above, the synthesis of carbon dots may be a novel method [21,22].

Allium macrostemon Bunge is a dried bulb from Allium macrostemon Bge. or Allium macrostemon Bge. In 2002, Allium macrostemon Bunge was listed by the Chinese Ministry of Health as one of the dual-use plants for medicinal and food purposes, which has the medical value of Chinese traditional medicine. The chemical composition [23] of Allium macrostemon Bunge mainly includes volatile oil, steroidal saponins, nitrogenous compounds, phenylpropanoids, flavonoids, polysaccharides and other components. Zhang [24] et al. isolated and purified a neutral polysaccharide from 60 % alcoholic fraction, consisting of Arabinose (Ara), Glucose (Glc) and Galactose (Gal). Similar to other natural polysaccharides, polysaccharide from Allium macrostemon Bunge (AMP) has good biodegradability, stability and low toxicity. In addition, AMP has in vitro antibacterial, tumor suppressive and in vivo neurotrophic activities. However, the bioactivity of AMP is not high and its applications are not many available.

In this work, AMP-CDs were synthesized for the first time by a one-step microwave method using AMP as relatively explicit compounds well as the main carbon source, L-cysteine as the main nitrogen and sulfur source, and phosphoric acid as the main phosphorus source, and the synthesis was optimized. In this paper, both AMPs and AMP-CDs were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR), fluorescence spectroscopy (FL), dynamic light scattering (DLS), etc. The stability of AMP-CDs was tested, and the in vitro antioxidant activities of AMP and AMP-CDs were evaluated. As shown in Scheme 1 , AMP-CDs can be caused by Cr6+ to fluorescence quenching, and the assay conditions were optimized to explore the mechanism of fluorescence quenching and applied to the Cr6+ recovery experiments. In addition, in vitro cell acute toxicity assay, hemolysis assay and cell imaging assay were also carried out.

Dried Allium macrostemon Bunge polysaccharides powder were extracted from laboratory (total sugar: 94.44 %). L-Cysteine was purchased from Tianjin Hiensiooptide Technology Co. Quinine sulfate was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Industrial Company (China). Ascorbic acid (VC) was purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). 2,2-Diphenyl-1-bitter hydrazine (DPPH), 2,2-Azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate) (ABTS) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Industrial Company (China). Potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) was purchased from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co. All other reagents used were of analytical grade, and all water used for analysis was distilled and deionized.

The morphology of samples were processed by scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Zeiss Sigma 300, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan). The valence state of of AMP-CDs were processed by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Nexsa, Thermo Scientific, USA). The Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by a FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker TENSOR27, Germany). The fluorescence spectra were determined by a fluorescence spectrometer (RF-5301PC, Hitachi). The fluorescence lifetimes of the samples were determined by a modular fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, Britain). UV spectra and absorption values of the samples were measured by UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-5500PC, Shanghai, China). Zeta potential was measured by Delsa Nano C nanoparticle size/zeta potential analyzer (Beckman Coulter, USA).

To a 250 mL open conical flask, 0.2 g of AMP and quantitative cysteine, mass ratio (1:1–1:9), and 200 μL phosphoric acid were weighed and measured. It was then added water to 20 mL, mixed thoroughly and put into a household microwave oven (G70D20CN1P-D2 (S0), Galanz, China) at 630 W for 160 s to obtain a reddish-brown viscous liquid. A small amount of water was added for dispersion, and the resulting liquid was passed through a 0.45 μm filter membrane followed by a 0.22 μm filter membrane, dialyzed for 24 h (MWCO 1000 Da) and then freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain dried AMP-CDs. The synthesis was optimized in terms of the mass ratio of AMP and cysteine, microwave power and time.

The fluorescence intensities of quinine sulfate and AMP-CDs were measured separately under the same excitation conditions, and the quantum yield [25] of AMP-CDs were calculated according to the following equation:
(1)
QYc =
QY s
I c
/
I s
η c
2
/
η s
2
A s
/
A c

QYc and QYs denote the quantum yield of AMP-CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively; Ic and Is denote the integrated fluorescence intensities of AMP-CDs and quinine sulfate at the same excitation wavelength, respectively; ηc and ηs denote the solvent refractive indices of AMP-CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively; As and Ac denote the UV absorbance of AMP-CDs and quinine sulfate at the same excitation wavelength, respectively.

The fluorescence spectra and intensities of AMP-CDs were measured at different excitation wavelengths (300-400 nm), different pH (1–13), NaCl concentration (0-2 M), UV irradiation time (0-60 min), concentration of itself (0.5–2.5 mg/mL), solvent and storage time (0-14D), respectively [26].

The solutions of different concentrations of samples (200–2000 μg/mL) and DPPH anhydrous ethanol solution (0.1 mmol/L) were mixed thoroughly at a volume ratio of 3:1 and left to react for 30 min at room temperature and protected from light, and the absorbance was measured at 517 nm [27]. Vc was used as a positive control, and the DPPH clearance was calculated according to the following formula, and the IC50 (μg/mL) was calculated by Graph Pad.
(2)
DPPH radical scavenging activity
%
=
A c
−
A a
+
A b
/
A c
× 100
Aa is the absorbance of sample mixed with DPPH, Ab is the absorbance of sample solution, and Ac is the absorbance of DPPH solution.

The mixture was mixed volumetrically with 7.4 mmol/L ABTS diammonium salt solution and 2.6 mmol/L K2S2O8 solution, and the mixture was placed at 4 °C and protected from light for 12-16 h. The mixture was diluted with water to obtain an absorbance of 0.70 ± 0.02 at 734 nm to obtain ABTS + free radical working solution. The solutions of different concentrations of samples (Vc and AMP-CDs solution concentration range: 2.5–25 μg/mL; AMP sample solution concentration range: 200–5000 μg/mL) and ABTS + free radical working solution were mixed thoroughly in equal volume 1:1 and left to react for 6 min at room temperature and protected from light, and the absorbance was measured at 734 nm [28]. Vc was used as a positive control. The ABTS clearance was calculated according to the following formula, and the IC50 (μg/mL) was calculated by Graph Pad.
(3)
ABTS ˙
+
radical scavenging activity
%
=
A c
−
A a
+
A b
/
A c
× 100
Aa is the absorbance of the sample mixed with ABTS, Ab is the absorbance of the sample solution, and Ac is the absorbance of the ABTS solution.

1 mL of 2 mmol/L salicylic acid ethanol solution was mixed with 2 mL of different concentrations of sample solution (200–2000 μg/mL) and 1 mL of 2 mmol/L FeSO4 solution at room temperature for 6 min in the dark. Then, 1 mL of 2.2 mmol/L H2O2 solution was added and mixed well, and left to react for 30 min at 37 °C in the dark. The absorbance was measured at 510 nm [29]. Vc was used as positive control. The IC50 (μg/mL) was calculated using Graph Pad and the ·OH scavenging rate was calculated according to the following formula:
(4)
Hydroxyl radical scavenging activity
%
=
A c
−
A a
+
A b
/
A c
× 100
Aa is the absorbance of the mixture with sample, Ab is the absorbance of the mixture with sample but not with H2O2, and Ac is the absorbance of the mixture without sample.

The AMP-CDs were configured into a solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL, and 0.15 mM of different metal ion solutions (including Mg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cr6+, Ni+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Hg+, Pb2+, n = 3) were added; the selectivity of Cr6+ sensing was confirmed by adding other metal ion stock solutions (including Mg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Ni+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Hg+, Pb2+, n = 3), and the fluorescence spectra of the samples were measured after 20 min. The concentration of AMP-CDs solution (1.0 mg/mL) was fixed, and the detection conditions were optimized in terms of pH (3.5–8.5), reaction time (5–30 min) and reaction temperature (5–45 °C).

Samples of lake water, tap water, rainwater, soil, mint leaves and basil leaves were collected on campus, and the liquid samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and the solid samples were ultrasonically dispersed with water (freshly picked mint and basil leaves were grinded) and filtered through medium-speed filter paper and then through a 0.22 μm membrane. The optimized detection method under item 2.8 was performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility assay and cellular imaging of AMP-CDs

ce:section.ce:para:
4T1 cells were inoculated in 96-well plates at a density of 8 × 103 cells/mL, incubated for 24 h, and then incubated with samples previously dissolved in PBS at different concentrations (12.5–400 μg/mL) for 24 h. Subsequently, 10 μL MTT solution was added and incubation was continued for 4 h before termination. Add 200 μL DMSO to each well, incubate for 10 min at 37 °C on a shaker, measure the absorbance of each well at 490 nm with an enzyme marker, and calculate the cell viability according to the following formula.
(5)
Cell viability
%
=
A sample
/
A control
× 100
Asample is the absorbance of sample group, Acontrol is the absorbance of control group without sample treatment.

Animals were treated in accordance with protocols evaluated and approved by the Ethics Committee of Guangdong Pharmaceutical University. Rats were finally euthanized by cervical dislocation. Blood was obtained from healthy rats, the serum was washed three times with saline, centrifuged at 1200 r/min for 5 min each time, and then diluted to 2 % erythrocyte suspension. Different concentrations of AMP-CDs solution were mixed with 2 % erythrocyte suspension in equal volumes, incubated at 37 °C for 1.5 h, and the absorbance was measured at 546 nm by microplate reader, and the hemolysis rate was calculated according to the following formula.
(6)
HR
%
=
A sample
−
A negative
/
A positive
−
A negative
× 100
Asample, Apositive, and Anegative are the absorbance of sample-treated, distilled water-treated, and saline-treated groups, respectively.

3 × 105 4T1 and RAW264.7 cells were inoculated into 20 mm glass-bottom confocal culture dishes, respectively, and cultured in 1 mL of medium (4T1 cells in RPMI 1640 medium and RAW264.7 cells in DMEM medium), and incubated at 37 °C, 5 % CO2 and 21 % O2 for 24 h until the cells were fully apposed. Then, 200 μg/mL of AMP-CDs were added separately and the medium was discarded after 24 h. The samples were washed three times with PBS (pH = 7.4) and then, the fluorescence expression was observed by laser confocal fluorescence microscopy (CLSM) (FLUOVIEW FV3000, Olympus, Japan) with a 40× objective set at excitation wavelengths of 405 nm and 488 nm.

Univariate analysis of variance (ANOVA) analysis of all quantitative data (P < 0.05) was followed by the Duncan multiple range test, expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD) (SPSS 20.0, SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA). All figures were drawn using Origin 2018 (Origin Lab Co., Northampton, MA, USA). The datasets generated and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

As shown in Fig. 1(A-C), the RSM experiments are conducted in the ranges of 560-720 W, 1:3–1:7, and 150 s–170 s based on the results of three single-factor experiments of microwave power, mass ratio, and synthesis time, respectively, and the design analysis was performed by Design Expert 8. The analysis results are shown in Fig. 1D-F and Table S1, and the model R2 is 0.9988, indicating that the experimental method is reliable and can be used for the experimental prediction of the synthesis of AMP-CDs. The predicted optimal condition is: synthesis time 163.23 s, microwave power 650.25 W, mass ratio 1:4.73, and the predicted value of FL is 771.118. Considering the actual experimental conditions, the synthesis parameter is revised to: synthesis time 160 s, microwave power 630 W, mass ratio 1:4.7, and the actual value of FL is 781.21 ± 7.28. The measured value is close to the predicted value, indicating the method is scientifically feasible and suitable for the synthesis of AMP-CDs.

As shown in Fig. 2A, the AMP solid powder appeared as porous honeycomb under microscopy. The solid state of AMP-CDs (Fig. 2B) is subject to spontaneous aggregation to be adherent spheroid, so that the particle size scale of AMP-CDs is visible in SEM at the micrometer level; EDS (Fig. 2C, D) shows that the atomic ratios (%) C: N: O: P: S = 56.80:16.99:17.90:3.11:5.19. As shown in Fig. 2E, AMP-CDs is sphere-like with an average diameter of 2.34 ± 0.49 nm (Fig. 2F), and the lattice spacing of AMP-CDs shown in HR-TEM (Fig. 2E) is 0.20 nm, which is similar to that of graphene [30], indicating that AMP-CDs has a similar lattice structure.

The three elements N, S, P are successfully doped into AMP-CDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3A, the five peaks 132.54 eV, 162.50 eV, 284.67 eV, 400.37 eV, 531.51 eV in the total XPS spectrum correspond to P2p, S2p, C1s, N1s, O1s, and the atomic ratios (%) C: N: O: P: S = 54.66:6.94:31.17:1.46:5.77, respectively. In AMP-CDs, the two analytical means, XPS and EDS, show a discrepancy in the proportion of atoms, which may be due to the difference in the principle and scanning depth of the two detection methods. As shown in Fig. 3B, there are two peaks concerning about C1s, CC at 284.67 eV, CO at 287.42 eV. As shown in Fig. 3C, two peaks are also observed, CN at 398.59 eV, at C-N/N-C=O 400.37 eV. As shown in Fig. 3D, two peaks about O1s are observed, CO (Aromatic) at 530.14 eV, CO at 531.51 eV. As shown in Fig. 3E, one peak at 132.54 eV can be ascribed to P-O(C6H5)3. As shown in Fig. 3F, two peaks at 162.50 eV, 163.82 eV can be ascribed to C6H5-SH and C-S-C.

The FT-IR spectra is shown in Fig. 4A. The peaks at 3406 cm−1 and 3417 cm−1 correspond to the hydroxyl characteristic peaks, which are displaced in AMP-CDs compared to CDs, probably due to the hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups in AMP-CDs. 2936 cm−1 corresponds to the CH stretching vibration, which is slightly red-shifted to 2943 cm−1 in AMP-CDs. The peak at 2936 cm−1 corresponds to the CH stretching vibration, which is slightly shifted to 2943 cm−1 in AMP-CDs. Peak at 1642 cm−1 corresponds to the -CO-NH -or CO stretching vibration, shifted to 1631 cm−1 in AMP-CDs. Peak at 1071 cm−1 is caused by the glycosidic bond C-O-H and CO. The characteristic absorption peak at 933 cm−1 is attributed to 3,6-anhydrogalactose [31], and the 954 cm−1 peak is attributed to the out-of-plane bending of C-OH in the carboxylic acid group of the AMP-CDs structure. In addition, the 150 0 cm−1 peak corresponds to the stretching vibration of NH in the structure of AMP-CDs, the 1402 cm−1 peak is attributed to the stretching vibration of CN, the 1244 cm−1 peak is attributed to the stretching vibration of CS, and the 877 cm−1 peak is attributed to the stretching vibration of S-O-C.

As shown in Fig. 4B, there is no obvious UV absorption peak for AMP, and there is an obvious UV absorption peak at 330 nm for AMP-CDs, which can be attributed to the n-π* jump of CO. The maximum excitation wavelength of AMP-CDs is 380 nm, and the emission wavelength is located at 465 nm. As shown in the inset of Fig. 4B, the AMP-CDs solution is a light yellowish liquid under visible light and fluoresces blue under UV-365 nm exposure. In addition, the measured zeta potential value was −18.56 ± 0.45 mV (Fig. S1A, B), which shows negative electricity.

As shown in Fig. 4B, there is no obvious UV absorption peak for AMP, and there is an obvious UV absorption peak at 330 nm for AMP-CDs, which can be attributed to the n-π* jump of CO. The maximum excitation wavelength of AMP-CDs is 380 nm, and the emission wavelength is located at 465 nm. As shown in the inset of Fig. 4B, the AMP-CDs solution is a light yellowish liquid under visible light and fluoresces blue under UV-365 nm exposure. In addition, the measured zeta potential value was −18.56 ± 0.45 mV (Fig. S1A, B), which shows negative electricity.

As shown in Fig. 4D and Tables S2, S3, the Int. FL. Intensity-Abs relationship plots for both quinine sulphate (dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4) and AMP-CDs are well linear with their slopes and R2 of 1,028,090.00, 0.9997 and 312,031.05, 0.9989, respectively. The QY (%) of AMP-CDs is calculated to be 16.4 %. In addition, as shown in Fig. 4C, with the increase of excitation wavelength (300-400 nm), the intensity of the emission peaks gradually enhance and then decrease and show a significant red-shift, which may be related to the complexity of the excited states on the surface of carbon dots and the different positions of the emitted holes, showing a certain excitation wavelength dependence.

As shown in Fig. S1C, within pH = 1–13, the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs starts to decrease after pH > 5, and a sudden decrease occurs after pH > 7. As shown in Fig. S1C, AMP-CDs fluoresces blue under UV-365 nm exposure at pH = 1, consistent with that in aqueous solution, and blue-green fluorescence at pH = 13. These indicate that AMP-CDs retain good fluorescence intensity in the normal pH range of human body. As shown in Fig. S1D, the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs remains essentially unchanged within the NaCl concentration (0-2 M), showing good salt resistance. As shown in Fig. S1E, the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs remains essentially unchanged within 0–60 min of UV irradiation, showing good resistance to photobleaching. As shown in Fig. S1F, the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs reaches the strongest at its own concentration of 1.5 mg/mL, with an overall trend of increasing and then decreasing. As shown in Fig. S1G, the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs reaches the highest in water, and with the decrease of solvent polarity, AMP-CDs is slightly soluble or insoluble in acetonitrile, ethyl acetate and cyclohexane, indicating that the surface of AMP-CDs is rich in hydrophilic groups, which is consistent with the analysis of FT-IR and XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. S1H, AMP-CDs maintains >60 % of fluorescence intensity during a storage time of 14 days. These results suggest that AMP-CDs have great potential for practical applications.

As shown in Fig. S1C, within pH = 1–13, the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs starts to decrease after pH > 5, and a sudden decrease occurs after pH > 7. As shown in Fig. S1C, AMP-CDs fluoresces blue under UV-365 nm exposure at pH = 1, consistent with that in aqueous solution, and blue-green fluorescence at pH = 13. These indicate that AMP-CDs retain good fluorescence intensity in the normal pH range of human body. As shown in Fig. S1D, the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs remains essentially unchanged within the NaCl concentration (0-2 M), showing good salt resistance. As shown in Fig. S1E, the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs remains essentially unchanged within 0–60 min of UV irradiation, showing good resistance to photobleaching. As shown in Fig. S1F, the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs reaches the strongest at its own concentration of 1.5 mg/mL, with an overall trend of increasing and then decreasing. As shown in Fig. S1G, the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs reaches the highest in water, and with the decrease of solvent polarity, AMP-CDs is slightly soluble or insoluble in acetonitrile, ethyl acetate and cyclohexane, indicating that the surface of AMP-CDs is rich in hydrophilic groups, which is consistent with the analysis of FT-IR and XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. S1H, AMP-CDs maintains >60 % of fluorescence intensity during a storage time of 14 days. These results suggest that AMP-CDs have great potential for practical applications.

As shown in Fig. 5A, the maximum clearance of DPPH by Vc, AMP-CDs and AMP are 78.80 %, 77.11 % and 19.61 % in the concentration range of 200–2000 μg/mL, respectively. As shown in Table 1 , the calculated IC50 of AMP-CDs and AMP are 10.240 μg/mL and 1.313 × 103 μg/mL, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 5B, the maximum clearance of ABTS by Vc and AMP-CDs are 99.83 % and 99.96 %, respectively, in the concentration range of 2.5–25 μg/mL. The maximum clearance of ABTS by AMP is 89.33 % at a concentration of 200 μg/mL. After calculation, the IC50 of AMP-CDs and AMP are 7.265 μg/mL and 1.654 × 103 μg/mL, respectively (Table 1).

As shown in Fig. 5C, the maximum clearance of OH by Vc, AMP-CDs and AMP are 100.71 %, 88.67 % and 14.59 % in the concentration range of 200–2000 μg/mL, respectively. After calculation, the IC50 of AMP-CDs and AMP are 574.730 μg/mL and 6.127 × 103 μg/mL, respectively (Table 1).

These results indicates that the in vitro antioxidant activity of AMP was substantially enhanced after synthesis into AMP-CDs compared with AMP, which has positive implications and expands the application of polysaccharides.

In view of the detection potential of AMP-CDs for Cr6+, it is necessary to optimize the detection conditions. As shown in Fig. 6A-C, the RSM experiments were carried out using three ranges of pH = 5.5–7.5, 20-30 min, and 20–30 °C, respectively, based on the results of three single-factor experiments of assay pH, time, and temperature, and the design analysis was performed using Design Expert 8. The analysis results are shown in Fig. 6D-F and Table S4, and the R2 of this model is 0.9980, indicating that the experimental method is reliable and can be used for the experimental prediction of the detection of Cr6+ by AMP-CDs. The predicted optimal detection conditions are: detection pH = 5.89, reaction time 20 min, reaction temperature 25 °C, and the predicted value of F0-F is 393.21. Considering the actual experimental conditions, the detection parameters are modified to: detection pH = 5.9, reaction time 20 min, and reaction temperature 25 °C, and the actual value of F0-F is obtained as 386.83 ± 4.02. The measured values are close to the predicted values, indicating that this method is scientifically feasible and applicable to the detection of Cr6+ by AMP-CDs.

As shown in Fig. 7A, after the addition of 0.15 mM different metal ion solutions, the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs decreases below 60 % after the addition of Cr6+ and still maintains >90 % fluorescence intensity for other metal ions. Competition experiments were conducted by adding 0.15 mM Cr6+ to each solution (blue bars, Fig. 7A). The results show that the presence of other metal ions slightly increases the fluorescence intensity of the detection system and does not significantly affect the detection of Cr6+ by AMP-CDs. Fe2+ and Cr6+ will undergo a redox reaction to produce Fe3+ and Cr3+, but there will still be some Cr6+ remaining, so the fluorescence intensity of this group will be higher than that of all but the blank group. It shows that AMP-CDs have good selectivity for Cr6+, and further in-depth experiments can be conducted.

To investigate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of AMP-CDs on Cr6+, the fluorescence lifetime, UV and FL spectra, and the linear relationship of AMP-CDs on Cr6+ at different temperatures are measured. As shown in Fig. 7B, there is a slight increase in the absorption of AMP-CDs at UV 200-300 nm after the addition of Cr6+, and there is a partial overlap between the UV absorption pattern and the fluorescence excitation pattern of AMP-CDs (Fig. 3B), which might have an internal filtering effect. As shown in Fig. 7C, the average fluorescence lifetime is τ0 = 8.9995 ± 0.0433 ns (τ1 = 1.0199 ns (10.94 %), τ2 = 9.9797 ns (89.06 %)) of AMP-CDs, and after the addition of Cr6+, the average fluorescence lifetime becomes τ0 = 8.7014 ± 0.0466 ns (τ1 = 1.2250 ns (14.95 %), τ2 = 10.0156 ns (85.05 %)), which shows little change in the fluorescence lifetime.

As shown in Fig. 7D, with the increase of Cr6+ concentration (0–500 μM), the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs decreases and there is a red shift of the emission peak (Fig. 7E), with good linear relationship of F0/F on Cr6+ (R2 = 0.9979), and the LOD is calculated to be 5.25 μM (3σ/K 0), which is not outstanding compared to that of the relevant work (Table 2 ), but it is sufficient enough for subsequent testing of the actual samples. As shown in Fig. 7F, the slopes of the linear equations of F0/F for Cr6+ at 288.15 K, 298.15 K, and 308.15 K are 0.0039, 0.0042, and 0.0044, respectively, which increase slightly. However, the calculated Kq value of 4.3685 × 1011 L/(mol-s) at a temperature of 288.15 K is obtained by the following Stern-Volmer equation [32,33], which is still much greater than the maximum dynamic quenching constant K = 2 × 1010 L/(mol-s), so the combined result, AMP-CDs quenching mechanism for Cr6+ consists mainly of static quenching [34] and internal filtering effect [35].
(7)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
Cr
6 +
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
Cr
6 +
= 0.0039
Cr
6 +
+ 1.0080

F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs without the addition of metal ion Cr6+; F represents the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs after the addition of metal ion Cr6+; KSV is the Stern-Volmer dynamic quenching constant; Kq is the dynamic fluorescence quenching rate constant; τ0 represents the average lifetime of fluorescent molecules in the absence of Cr6+.

To further investigate the practicality of AMP-CDs for Cr6+ detection, recovery experiments are designed [41] including lake water, rainwater, tap water, soil turbidity, crushed mint leaves and basil leaves. As shown in Table 3 , overall, relative recoveries ranges from 86.70 to 119.14 % with RSDs ranging from 0.59 to 8.19 %, respectively. These results indicate that AMP-CDs have good reproducibility and accuracy for the detection of Cr6+ in natural environmental water samples and solid samples, and are feasible as probes for the detection of Cr6+.

Both acute cytotoxicity and hemolysis rate are important indicators to examine the biosafety of drugs [42]. As shown in Fig. 8A, within the concentration of AMP-CDs (12.5–400 μg/mL), 4T1 cells remain >80 % viable. As shown in Fig. 8B, within the concentration of AMP-CDs (25–400 μg/mL), the hemolysis rate of erythrocytes shows a trend from rise to decline, and the hemolysis rates were all below 0.8 %. These results suggest that AMP-CDs have good biosafety and can be further subjected to biological experiments.

To further explore the biological applications of AMP-CDs, cell imaging experiments are carried out [43]. Long wavelength luminescence carbon dots do have advantages such as less background interference, however, they are difficult to synthesize with dangerous high temperature and pressure and long test cycles, and are not safe nor as desirable in practical applications [44–46]. On the other hand, blue light emission carbon dots can also be widely used for cell imaging [47–49], moreover, the blue light emission carbon dots in this work were synthesized by an efficient and safe microwave method. Observed with laser confocal microscope, as shown in Fig. 8C-J, AMP-CDs can successfully enter into 4T1 cells and RAW264.7 cells and be imaged clearly. The experiments show that AMP-CDs have low toxicity to cells, can easily pass through cell membranes to enter cells for fluorescence imaging, and have potential as biofluorescent probes.

In this paper, AMP-CDs with good optical stability, small nano size and high quantum yield are synthesized in one step by microwave treatment with only 160 s for the first time. A comprehensive characterization of AMP-CDs is carried out to determine the surface functional groups of AMP-CDs. In vitro antioxidant activity experiments show that the scavenging ability of AMP-CDs for DPPH, ABTS and ·OH is significantly higher than that of AMP. Selectivity experiments prove that AMP-CDs has good selective detection ability to Cr6+, and the detection system has sufficient anti-interference ability. Further experiments reveal that Cr6+ can effectively lead to the fluorescence quenching of AMP-CDs and the quenching follows static quenching mechanism and internal filtering effect with low detection limits. As shown by the recovery experiments, satisfying recovery results of detection of Cr6+ in environmental and food samples are obtained. In addition, in vitro cytotoxicity and hemolytic activity assays show that both cytotoxicity and hemolysis rates of AMP-CDs are low, and AMP-CDs can be clearly imaged intracellularly. In conclusion, carbon dots synthesis as a novel modification approach of natural polysaccharides broadens the application of medicinal foods, and AMP-CDs are expected to be used as antioxidants, sensitive and selective fluorescent sensors of Cr6+ with good biosafety in environmental and biological scenarios for both medicinal and metal ion detection.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
(A) EOS plot and (B) mobility distribution of AMP-CDs. (C-E, F-H) Effect of pH (1–13), ionic strength of NaCl (0–2.0 mol/L), the irradiation time of a xenon lamp on, concentration (0.5–2.5 mg/mL) of AMP-CDs, different solution, storage time (0-14D) on the fluorescence intensity of AMP-CDs (a-e: P < 0.05; n = 3).
Fig. S1
Supplementary tables
Image 1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110295.
